scenes.title: How Python's GIL Works
scenes.subtitle: Why Python threads don't always run in parallel.
scenes.label: Scene 1 / 10

scenes.title: The Intuition
scenes.subtitle: Multiple threads compete for a single lock before executing Python code.
scenes.label: Scene 2 / 10

scenes.title: What Is the GIL?
scenes.subtitle: A mutex that prevents multiple threads from executing Python bytecode at once.
scenes.label: Scene 3 / 10

scenes.title: Thread Switching
scenes.subtitle: The GIL is periodically released and re-acquired, allowing thread interleaving.
scenes.label: Scene 4 / 10

scenes.title: CPU-Bound Work
scenes.subtitle: Compute-heavy threads waste time waiting for the GIL instead of running in parallel.
scenes.label: Scene 5 / 10

scenes.title: Why Does the GIL Exist?
scenes.subtitle: It protects shared interpreter state and simplifies reference counting.
scenes.label: Scene 6 / 10

scenes.title: I/O Changes Everything
scenes.subtitle: The GIL is released during blocking I/O, allowing other threads to run.
scenes.label: Scene 7 / 10

scenes.title: Why Threads Still Help
scenes.subtitle: Threads improve throughput for I/O-bound workloads despite the GIL.
scenes.label: Scene 8 / 10

scenes.title: Multiprocessing
scenes.subtitle: Each process gets its own interpreter and GIL, enabling true parallelism.
scenes.label: Scene 9 / 10

scenes.title: Final Summary
scenes.subtitle: The GIL is a design trade-off between simplicity and parallelism.
scenes.label: Scene 10 / 10
